laws.title: IPC Section 354 — Assault or Criminal Force on Woman with Intent to Outrage Modesty
laws.description: Assault or criminal force to any woman, intending to outrage or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby outrage her modesty.
laws.punishment: Imprisonment of either description for a term which shall NOT be less than 1 year, but which may extend to 5 years, AND fine.
key_facts: Minimum punishment: 1 year imprisonment (non-bailable)
Maximum punishment: 5 years imprisonment + fine
Cognizable and non-bailable offence
FIR can be registered at any police station
Attempt to commit this offence is also punishable under Section 354A, 354B, 354C, 354D

sub_sections.354A: Sexual harassment and punishment (1–3 years or fine or both)
sub_sections.354B: Assault/force to woman with intent to disrobe (3–7 years + fine)
sub_sections.354C: Voyeurism — watching/capturing a woman in private act (1–3 years first offence; 3–7 years repeat)
sub_sections.354D: Stalking (1–3 years first offence; 3–5 years repeat)

laws.title: IPC Section 376 — Punishment for Rape
laws.description: Whoever commits rape shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment of either description for a term which shall NOT be less than 10 years, but which may extend to imprisonment for life.
laws.punishment: Minimum 10 years rigorous imprisonment, extendable to life imprisonment + fine. In aggravated cases: minimum 20 years to life.
key_facts: Minimum 10 years rigorous imprisonment
If victim is below 16 years: minimum 20 years (Section 376AB)
Gang rape (376D): minimum 20 years to life
Rape by police/public servant: minimum 10 years
POCSO Act 2012 applies when victim is a child (below 18)
Victim identity must NOT be disclosed — punishable under Section 228A IPC
Medical examination of victim must be done by female doctor
FIR must be registered. Cannot be refused by police.
Free legal aid available to victim
process: Step 1: Call Police 100 or go to nearest police station — FIR will be registered
Step 2: Medical examination within 24 hours (preserve evidence)
Step 3: Statement to magistrate under Section 164 CrPC
Step 4: Trial in Fast Track Court (mandatory in rape cases)
Step 5: Victim compensation under Section 357A CrPC

laws.title: IPC Section 498A — Cruelty by Husband or Relatives
laws.description: Whoever, being the husband or a relative of the husband of a woman, subjects such woman to cruelty shall be punished. Cruelty includes willful conduct likely to drive woman to suicide, grave injury to life/limb/health, or harassment over dowry demands.
laws.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years AND fine.
key_facts: Cognizable, non-bailable, and non-compoundable offence
Both husband AND his relatives can be charged
Cruelty includes: physical abuse, mental harassment, dowry demands
FIR can be filed by wife OR her relatives
Accused can be arrested without warrant
Police must complete investigation within 60 days
Often used together with Domestic Violence Act 2005

laws.title: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
laws.act: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
laws.description: Provides protection to women from domestic violence — physical, sexual, verbal, emotional, and economic abuse — by husband or live-in partner or their family members.
key_rights: Protection Order: Court can prohibit abuser from committing any act of domestic violence
Residence Order: Woman cannot be evicted from shared household
Monetary Relief: Maintenance, medical expenses, loss of earnings
Custody Order: Temporary custody of children
Compensation Order: For injuries caused by domestic violence
laws.who_can_apply: Any woman who is or has been in a domestic relationship (wife, live-in partner, sister, mother, daughter)
how_to_apply: Step 1: Contact Protection Officer (in every district)
Step 3: Magistrate court can pass protection order within 3 days
Step 4: Emergency protection available same day
laws.punishment: Breach of protection order — imprisonment up to 1 year OR fine up to Rs.20,000 or both

laws.title: Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961
laws.description: Makes giving and taking of dowry a criminal offence in India. Also prohibits demanding dowry before, at, or after marriage.
key_provisions: Section 3: Giving or taking dowry — imprisonment 5 years + fine Rs.15,000 or value of dowry (whichever higher)
Section 4: Demanding dowry — imprisonment 6 months to 2 years + fine up to Rs.10,000
Section 304B IPC: Dowry Death — if woman dies within 7 years of marriage due to dowry harassment — imprisonment 7 years to life
Section 113B Evidence Act: Presumption of dowry death — burden of proof on husband/relatives
important_facts: Both giving AND taking dowry is illegal
Demanding dowry is also illegal (even if not given)
Stridhan (woman's own property/gifts) is NOT dowry — woman has full rights to it
Dowry death (304B) is non-bailable, cognizable offence
Complaint can be filed by woman, her parents, or any relative

laws.title: Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 — POSH Act
laws.act: Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace Act, 2013
laws.description: Provides protection to women from sexual harassment at the workplace and establishes mechanism for redressal of complaints.
what_is_sexual_harassment: Demand or request for sexual favours
Any other unwelcome physical, verbal or non-verbal conduct of sexual nature
key_provisions: Every employer with 10+ employees must constitute Internal Complaints Committee (ICC)
ICC must have a woman as Presiding Officer
At least 50% members of ICC must be women
Complaint must be filed within 3 months of incident
ICC inquiry must complete within 90 days
Employer can be fined Rs.50,000 for first offence; licence cancellation for repeat
process: Step 1: File written complaint to ICC of your organization
Step 2: If no ICC exists or employer ignores — file with Local Complaints Committee (LCC) at District level
Step 4: If proven — punishment includes warning, fine, termination
Step 5: Can also file criminal complaint under IPC 354A
laws.punishment: Employer fine: Rs.50,000 first offence; licence revocation for repeat offence

laws.title: Right to Information Act, 2005 — RTI Act
laws.description: Gives every Indian citizen the right to request information from any public authority. Promotes transparency and accountability in government.
key_rights: Any citizen of India can file RTI
Can request copies of documents, records, files
Can request certified sample of any government material
process: Step 1: Write RTI application on plain paper or online at rtionline.gov.in
Step 2: Address to Public Information Officer (PIO) of the concerned department
Step 3: Pay Rs.10 application fee (free for BPL card holders)
Step 4: PIO must respond within 30 days (48 hours if life/liberty at stake)
Step 5: If denied or no reply — file First Appeal within 30 days
Step 6: Second Appeal to Central/State Information Commission
exemptions: Information that would endanger life of informant
laws.penalty: PIO can be fined Rs.250 per day of delay (max Rs.25,000) for non-compliance
laws.helpline: RTI Online Portal: rtionline.gov.in, Central Information Commission: 011-26186536

laws.title: Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 — POCSO Act
laws.act: Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012
laws.description: Protects children below 18 years from sexual abuse and exploitation. Provides for speedy trial through Special Courts.
key_provisions: Child = any person below 18 years of age
Penetrative sexual assault (rape) on child — minimum 10 years to life + fine
Aggravated penetrative assault (by relative, position of trust) — minimum 20 years to life
Sexual assault — minimum 3 years to 5 years + fine
Sexual harassment of child — up to 3 years + fine
Child pornography — 5–7 years + fine
laws.mandatory_reporting: Any person (including doctor, teacher) who has knowledge of POCSO offence MUST report to police — failure is punishable by 6 months imprisonment
special_features: Evidence recorded by female officer if victim is girl
Speedy trial mandatory in Special Court

laws.title: IPC Section 354D — Stalking & IT Act — Cyber Crimes
laws.act: Indian Penal Code (Section 354D) and Information Technology Act, 2000
laws.section: Section 354D IPC, Section 66E and 67A IT Act
laws.description: Stalking (online or physical) and cyber crimes against women are criminal offences under IPC and IT Act.
key_provisions: Section 354D IPC: Stalking — following, contacting, monitoring online without consent — 1–3 years (1st offence), 3–5 years (repeat)
Section 66E IT Act: Capturing/publishing private images without consent — up to 3 years + fine Rs.2 lakh
Section 67A IT Act: Publishing sexually explicit material — 5 years + fine Rs.10 lakh (first offence)
Section 66C: Identity theft / impersonation — 3 years + fine Rs.1 lakh
Section 66D: Cheating by impersonation — 3 years + fine Rs.1 lakh
how_to_report: Step 1: File complaint at Cyber Crime Portal: cybercrime.gov.in
Step 3: Report to nearest police station (mention IT Act sections)
Step 4: Preserve evidence — screenshots, call logs, messages
Step 5: Report offending profile to social media platform

laws.title: Free Legal Aid in India — Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987
laws.description: Every Indian citizen has the right to free legal aid if they cannot afford a lawyer. NALSA (National Legal Services Authority) provides free legal services.
who_qualifies: Victims of natural disasters, mass disasters
Persons with annual income below Rs.3 lakh (varies by state)
how_to_get: Contact District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) in your district

metadata.disclaimer: This knowledge base contains verified Indian laws for RAG-based retrieval. Laws may be amended. Always verify with official government sources or consult a qualified lawyer.
sources: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace Act, 2013
Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012
